title: Estimating and testing investment-based asset pricing models
abstract: Investment-based asset pricing models typically predict a close link between a firm’s stock return and its characteristics at any point in time. Yet, previous studies have primarily focused on the weaker prediction that this link holds on average, finding substantial empirical support. We show how to incorporate the time-series predictions in the estimation and testing of investment-based models using the generalized method of moments. We find that standard specifications of investment-based models with one physical capital input fail to match the time series properties of stock returns in the data, and discuss the implications of the findings for future research.

author.Frederico Belo*: INSEAD, France|||Center for Economic and Policy Research (CEPR), United Kingdom
author.Yao Deng: University of Connecticut, United States of America
author.Juliana Salomao: University of Minnesota, Carlson School of Management, United States of America|||Center for Economic and Policy Research (CEPR), United Kingdom|||National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER), United States of America

article.1. Introduction: The neoclassical investment-based asset pricing model predicts that a firm’s equilibrium stock return is closely related to its characteristics at any point in time (Cochrane, 1991, Restoy and Rockinger, 1994). Despite this strong time-series prediction, most of the structural work in investment-based asset pricing to date, tests the model by the generalized methods of moments (GMM) using the weaker prediction that a firm’s stock return and its characteristics should be related on average. Following this procedure, Liu et al. (2009) (henceforth LWZ) shows that a standard neoclassical investment-based model with one (physical) capital input and homogeneous of degree one technology matches surprisingly well the cross-sectional dispersion in average stock returns of several portfolio sorts. In this paper, we show how to incorporate the stronger time-series predictions in the estimation and testing of investment-based models using GMM. We find that standard specifications of the investment-based model with one (physical) capital input, including LWZ, fail to capture the time-series properties of stock returns in the data, and we discuss the implications of the findings for future research.We first apply our estimation approach to the standard specification of the investment-based model with one (physical) capital input and homogeneous of degree one technology, as the one examined in LWZ. This model predicts that a firm’s realized stock return should be equal to its (levered) realized investment return, which is a function of firm-characteristics such as investment rate and output. As in a standard nonlinear least squares estimation approach, we add the average squared distance between stock returns and model-implied investment returns at each point in time as a new set of target moment conditions in the GMM estimation of the model, which we label as time-series moments. In addition, as in previous work, we also consider cross-sectional moments, that is, the model’s prediction that stock returns and model-implied investment returns should be equal on average. We follow LWZ and estimate the baseline model at the portfolio-level, using ten book-to-market portfolios, to reduce the impact of estimation noise. The estimation targets twenty moments: ten cross-sectional moments as in previous studies, and our novel ten time-series moments. To understand the role of the two set of moments on the results, we investigate how the estimates and model fit change when we vary the relative weight of the two set of moments in the GMM weighting matrix.The estimation results of the baseline investment-based model can be summarized as follows. First, consistent with LWZ, when we only use cross-sectional moments in the GMM estimation, the model matches these cross-sectional moments very well, with low cross-sectional pricing errors (about 1.4% per year) and a high cross-sectional R2 (henceforth XS−R2) of the scatter plot of average portfolio-level stock returns against average portfolio-level estimated investment returns, of 71%. The time-series fit of model is very poor, however, with a negative time-series R2 (henceforth TS−R2), measured as the time-series R2 of a linear projection of the realized stock return against the estimated investment return, of −93% across portfolios.We conclude that when estimating the baseline investment-based model using the cross-sectional moments only, the model fails to capture its time series implications.Second, our estimation approach uncovers a novel trade-off between cross-sectional fit and time-series fit in the baseline investment-based model: the model cannot fit both sets of moments simultaneously. As we increase the relative weight of the time-series moments in the GMM estimation of the model to improve its time-series fit, the XS−R2 deteriorates significantly from 71% when only cross-sectional moments are used in the estimation, to −134% when only time-series moments are used in the estimation. As expected, the time-series fit of the model improves when the weight of the time-series moments increases. However, and perhaps surprisingly, the model’s time-series fit remains poor even when only the time-series moments are used in the estimation, with a TS−R2 of −4%. That is, the baseline investment-based model is unable to capture the time-series behavior of stock returns in the data even when the estimation is designed to maximize its time series fit.We then investigate two potential concerns with the standard empirical procedures and with the data that might be responsible for the poor time-series fit of the baseline investment-based model. First, we investigate the role of possible issues in the portfolio aggregation for the results. As noted in Belo et al. (2022) (henceforth BGSV), and Gonçalves et al. (2020) (henceforth GXZ), the portfolio-level aggregation procedure in LWZ suffers from an aggregation bias (we discuss the source of this aggregation bias in detail in Section 6.1). Following BGSV and GXZ, we construct bias-free portfolio-level investment returns. Using simulated data from a calibrated version of the baseline investment-based model, we confirm that, in theory, the TS−R2 should be perfect when the portfolio-level investment returns are properly aggregated, and that the LWZ biased portfolio-level aggregation does lead to very low TS−R2. However, when we re-estimate the model in the real data with a proper bias-free portfolio-level aggregation, the fit of the model in the time series remains very poor, TS−R2=−17%, even when the estimation focuses only on the time-series moments to maximize its time-series fit. Thus, the aggregation bias in LWZ does not appear to be the main cause for the inability of the baseline investment-based model to capture the time series behavior of stock returns in the data.Second, we address the concern that asset prices and quantity (e.g. investment) data might be misaligned. For example, stock prices (and hence stock returns) might respond instantaneously to aggregate shocks, whereas investment might take more time to adjust, in which case investment returns lag stock returns (see, for example, Lamont 2000 for a more formal analysis of this issue). To address this concern, we investigate the time-series fit of the model using time-smoothed data, that is, (annualized) 5-year compounded returns. If the misalignment in the data is relatively short lived, the misalignment between stock and investment returns should be less pronounced at longer-horizons. We provide support for this conjecture using again simulated data from a calibrated version of the baseline investment-based model in which we parameterize the data misalignment and show that, in theory, the TS−R2 is indeed significantly higher when we use (annualized) compounded returns in the presence of data misalignment. However, in the real data, the TS−R2 using (annualized) 5-year compounded stock and investment returns remains negative, TS−R2=−11%. Thus, the misalignment in asset prices and real quantity data also does not appear to be the main cause for the inability of the baseline investment-based model to capture the time series behavior of stock returns in the data.Finally, we show how to extend our approach to evaluate the performance of a larger class of investment-based models in which the stock-investment return equality might not hold. In general, it is natural to expect investment-based models to imply a close link between a firm’s equilibrium stock return and its characteristics. The strength of this link can be estimated in any model by running simple time-series and cross-sectional linear regressions of stock returns on firm characteristics using simulated data. We can then compare these results with the corresponding estimation results in the real data to evaluate the model’s fit.We apply this flexible evaluation approach of investment-based models to two alternative model specifications with one (physical) capital input in which the stock-investment return equality does not hold. We consider the Lin and Zhang (2013) investment-based model with decreasing returns to scale, non convex and asymmetric adjustment costs, and operating fixed costs, and the Kogan and Papanikolaou (2013) non-adjustment-cost model with investment-specific shocks. Using simulated data aggregated at the portfolio-level, we document that these models also imply a strong (linear) relationship between equilibrium stock returns and firm-characteristics (here, marginal product of physical capital, firm size, and the current and lagged physical capital investment rates), both in the cross-section (XS−R2 above 96%) and in the time-series (TS−R2 above 84%). When applied to the real data, however, the estimated time-series relationship between realized stock returns and the same firm characteristics is weak (TS−R2 around 5%). Thus, the set of firm characteristics driving stock returns in these investment-based models with one (physical) capital input differs from those in the real data, indicating that important firm-characteristics might be missing from these models.Taken together, our findings highlight the importance of examining the time-series implications of investment-based models for proper model evaluation. We show that although standard specifications of investment-based asset pricing models with one (physical) capital input can match the cross section of average stock return quite well, these models struggle to match the time series of stock returns observed in the data.The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 discusses the related literature. Section 3 presents the neoclassical investment-based model. Section 4 describes the econometric methodology. Section 5 reports the estimation and tests results of the model. Section 6 investigates potential empirical reasons for the poor time-series fit. Section 7 proposes an extension of the approach that can be used to evaluate more general specifications of investment-based models in which the stock-investment return equality does not hold. Finally, Section 8 concludes and discusses the implications of our findings for future research in investment-based asset pricing. A separate appendix with additional results is available online.
article.2. Related literature: Our work is closely related to LWZ who first estimate the baseline neoclassical investment-based model on the cross-section of stock returns. Different from LWZ, our estimation procedure requires the model to also match the time series of the observed stock returns as close as possible (in the same spirit, BGSV requires the model to match the time series of valuation ratios), and not just on average.1 LWZ use their estimates to document that the implied stock and investment returns have low correlation, which they label a correlation puzzle. Similarly, the correlation puzzle is documented at the aggregate level in Kuehn (2009). We show that this puzzle persists even when the estimation is designed to maximize the time-series fit of the model. In addition, we show how to incorporate the time series implications of investment-based models directly in the estimation and evaluation of these models, and we discuss potential alternative empirical reasons for the observed poor time-series fit.Li et al. (2021) estimate an investment-based model with two capital inputs using firm-level data and Bayesian estimation methods. Different from our work, they focus only on a specification of the investment-based model in which the homogeneous of degree one assumption holds, and they estimate the model to match firm-level stock returns. In addition, and departing from baseline neoclassical investment-based models with stable technologies, they estimate industry-specific and time-varying technological parameters. They show that allowing for time-varying parameters of the firm’s technology helps to significantly improve the fit of the model in explaining the behavior of returns of several anomaly portfolios.GXZ document the aggregation bias in the original LWZ portfolio-level aggregation approach (see also BGSV and Zhang, 2017, for earlier discussions of this aggregation bias in LWZ). Our analysis shows that the aggregation bias alone cannot explain the poor fit of the one (physical) capital input investment-based model in the time series.Delikouras and Dittmar (2021) estimate and test standard investment-based models using GMM with cross-sectional moments and investment Euler equations, which requires the specification of a stochastic discount factor, and also find that the baseline investment-based model is unable to match both sets of moments jointly. Our paper shares the goal of testing investment-based models across a larger set of model implications, but differs in the approach. Our analysis focuses on the properties of the firm’s technology and does not require the specification of a stochastic discount factor (at least for the estimation of baseline model), thus avoiding the joint hypothesis testing problem. Naturally, the ultimate goal is to obtain a specification of firm’s technology and of the stochastic discount factor that simultaneously matches the cross-sectional, the time-series, and the investment Euler equations as in Delikouras and Dittmar (2021). However, focusing only on the properties of the firm’s technology allows us to narrow down how its specification alone affects the time-series and cross-sectional fit of the model. Our paper thus complements their approach by providing the first step (focus on firm’s technology) towards that goal.Finally, our work is related to the literature on estimation and testing of structural models in financial economics, and to the strand of the investment-based asset pricing literature that links firm characteristics to asset returns.2 We contribute to these literatures by improving the econometric methodology for estimating and testing investment-based models.
article.3. The neoclassical investment-based mod: We briefly present the baseline neoclassical investment-based model of the firm with one (physical) capital input and homogeneous of degree one technology as in LWZ. We use their notation whenever possible. Time is discrete and the horizon infinite. Firms choose costlessly adjustable inputs each period, taking their prices as given, to maximize operating profits (revenues minus expenditures on these inputs). Taking operating profits as given, firms choose investment and debt to maximize the market equity.Operating profits for firm i at time t are given by ΠKit,Xit, in which Kit is physical capital and Xit is a vector of exogenous aggregate and firm-specific shocks. We assume that this function exhibits constant returns to scale, that is, ΠKit,Xit=Kit∂ΠKit,Xit/∂Kit, and that firm i has a Cobb–Douglas production function. The marginal product of capital is given by ∂ΠKit,Xit/∂Kit=αYit/Kit, in which 0<α≤1 is the capital’s share in output and Yit is sales.Capital depreciates at an exogenous rate of δit, which is firm-specific and time-varying: (1)Kit+1=Iit+1−δitKit,where Iit is investment. Firms incur adjustment costs when investing. The adjustment costs function, denoted ΦIit,Kit, is increasing and convex in Iit, is decreasing in Kit, and has constant returns to scale in Iit and Kit. We use a standard quadratic functional form: (2)ΦIit,Kit=c2IitKit2Kit,where c>0 is the slope adjustment cost parameter.Firms finance investment with one-period debt. At the beginning of period t, firm i issues an amount of debt, denoted Bit+1, that must be repaid at the beginning of t+1. Let ritB denote the gross corporate bond return on Bit. We can write taxable corporate profits as operating profits minus depreciation, adjustment costs, and interest expense: ΠKit,Xit−δitKit−ΦIit,Kit−ritB−1Bit. Let τt denote the corporate tax rate. We define the payout of firm i as: (3)Dit≡1−τtΠKit,Xit−ΦIit,Kit−Iit+Bit+1−ritBBit+τtδitKit+τtritB−1Bit, where τtδitKit is the depreciation tax shield, and τtritB−1Bit is the interest tax shield.Let Mt+1 denote the stochastic discount factor from period t to t+1, which is correlated with the aggregate component of the productivity shock Xit. The firm chooses optimal capital investment and debt to maximize the cum-dividend market value of equity: (4)Vit≡maxIit+s,Kit+s+1,Bit+s+1s=0∞Et∑s=0∞Mt+sDit+s,subject to a transversality condition given by limT→∞EtMt+TBit+T+1=0.Firms’ equity value maximization implies that EtMt+1rit+1I=1, in which rit+1I is the investment return, defined as (5)rit+1I≡1−τt+1αYit+1Kit+1+c2Iit+1Kit+12+τt+1δit+1+1−δit+11+1−τt+1cIit+1Kit+11+1−τtcIitKit. The investment return is the ratio of the marginal benefits of investment at period t+1 to the marginal costs of investment at t.The first-order condition of maximizing Eq. (4) with respect to Bit+1 implies that EtMt+1rit+1Ba=1, in which rit+1Ba≡rit+1B−rit+1B−1τt+1 is the after-tax corporate bond return. Define Pit≡Vit−Dit as the ex-dividend equity value, rit+1S≡Pit+1+Dit+1/Pit as the stock return, and wit≡Bit+1/Pit+Bit+1 as the market leverage. Under constant returns to scale of the operating profit and adjustment cost functions (the Hayashi (1982) conditions), the investment return equals the weighted average of the stock return and the after-tax corporate bond return: (6)rit+1I=witrit+1Ba+1−witrit+1S.Equivalently, the stock return equals the levered investment return, denoted rit+1Iw: (7)rit+1S=rit+1Iw≡rit+1I−witrit+1Ba1−wit.
article.4. Econometric methodology: Section 4.1 describes the portfolio approach used to estimate the baseline model. Section 4.2 discusses the cross-sectional moment conditions used in prior studies, and presents our new time-series moment conditions. Section 4.3 describes the generalized method of moments (GMM) estimation methodology. Section 4.4 discusses the metrics used to evaluate the fit of the model.4.1. Portfolio approachEq. (7) establishes an exact relationship between a firm’s observed stock return and its model-implied levered investment return at each point in time. Using this equation with firm-level data to estimate the model parameters can be challenging because the firm-level data can be very noisy. Thus, we estimate the model parameters using portfolio-level moments as in LWZ. The use of portfolio-level moments, a common practice in the asset pricing literature, has several attractive features in our context. First, it allows us to reduce the noise in the firm-level data, and hence obtain more accurate parameter estimates and measures of model fit such as R2. Second, portfolio-level moments are less sensitive, and hence more stable, to firm entry and exit, and to missing firm-level observations. Finally, the use of portfolio-level moments allows us link our approach to the large empirical asset pricing literature, and to characterize the data in a more parsimonious manner as the number of portfolios is naturally smaller than the number of firms in the data.Following LWZ, we construct the portfolio-level investment returns as follows. We first separately compute each portfolio-level characteristic in the investment return formula given by Eq. (5) as Iit+1/Kit+1=∑j=1NitIijt+1/∑j=1NitKijt+1, Yit/Kit=∑j=1NitYijt/∑j=1NitKijt, etc, where a portfolio is indexed by i =1,…,N, a firm is indexed by j, and Nit is the number of firms in portfolio i at time t. We then plug each characteristic in the investment return formula given by Eq. (5) to compute the portfolio-level investment return. We label this portfolio aggregation approach as LWZ aggregation. We use ten book-to-market (BM) portfolios as test assets (we consider other test assets in the appendix), and describe the construction of the portfolios in the Data Section 5.1.4.2. Cross-sectional and time-series momentsWe estimate the investment-based model using the generalized method of moments (GMM). We define the moment conditions as follows. As in LWZ, we consider a set of cross-sectional moments implied by Eq. (7). In particular, this equation implies a weaker model prediction that stock returns should be equal to levered investment returns on average, (8)giXS=ETrit+1S−rit+1Iw=0,in which ET⋅ is the sample mean of the series in brackets. We can stack the previous moment for each portfolio into a column vector gXS, which we label as cross-sectional moment conditions.According to Eq. (7), the investment-based model has rich implications not only for the cross-section of average stock returns, but also for the time-series of stock returns because the equality between stock and (levered) investment returns should hold at any point in time, not just on average. Accordingly, we also require the estimation to match this equality at each point in time as closely as possible, by adding a new set of time-series moment conditions. Consistent with a standard nonlinear least squares estimation approach (henceforth NLLS), we specify the time-series moment condition for each portfolio as the time-series average of the squared differences between realized stock returns and levered investment returns: (9)giTS=ETrit+1S−rit+1Iw2=0.We can stack the previous moment for each portfolio into a column vector gTS, which we label as time-series moment conditions. Because these moments capture the fit of the model at each point in time, and not just on average, it arguably constitutes a higher bar for a candidate model to clear.3We note that we use the NLLS objective function (sum of squared residuals) as the target time-series moment for each portfolio. Under the null that the model is correctly specified, this moment should be zero, and hence it is a valid moment condition for GMM. Naturally, if there is some measurement error or other noise in the data, the moment will deviate from zero even if the model is correctly specified, but it should not deviate too much and the deviation should be random. The fact that the deviation might be different from zero due to noise might affect the interpretation of the chi-square tests for assessing the validity of the model (described in the next section), but this is not a concern in our analysis given that our focus is mostly on the interpretation of the model fit on economic grounds, with less emphasis on the statistical tests of the model. Finally, the use of NLLS objective function as the moment condition has the benefit of being parsimonious and easy to interpret.44.3. GMM estimationWe estimate the model parameters, θ≡(α,c), using one-step GMM to minimize three different set of moments: (i) a weighted average of giXS; (ii) a weighted average of giTS; or (iii) a weighted average of giXS and giTS. Specifically, we stack the cross-sectional and the time-series moment conditions in the matrix g≡gXS;gTS. We then estimate the model parameters θ by minimizing a weighted combination of the sample moments, denoted by gT: (10)minθgT′WgT,in which W is the prespecified weighting matrix. As discussed in Cochrane (2009), a prespecified weighting matrix forces the estimation and evaluation to pay attention to economically interesting moments. In our context, we use several different specifications of the weighting matrix, which vary on the relative weights of the cross-sectional and time-series moments in the estimation. If the model is correct, this choice of target moments should not affect the parameter estimates nor the fit of the model. The same set of model parameters should match both the cross-sectional and the time-series moments, because both are proper moment conditions and GMM is a consistent estimator.Specifically, W=I,0 indicates that we put identity weights on gXS and zero weights on gTS (cross-sectional moments only, which we label as Only XS in the tables). This is a special case of our methodology and identical to the GMM estimation in LWZ. When W=0,I, we assign identity weights on gTS and no weights on gXS (time-series moments only, which we label as Only TS in the tables). More generally, when W=[I,Z], we assign identity weights on gXS and positive weights Z on gTS (cross-sectional and time-series moments, which we label as Both XS and TS in the tables). Here, by increasing Z, we are putting relatively more weight on gTS over gXS, thus forcing the estimation to pay relatively more attention to the time-series features of the data.4.4. Model evaluationWe evaluate the fit of the investment-based model on economic grounds, with less emphasis on the statistical tests. As discussed in Cochrane (1991), the baseline investment-based model should be rejected at any level of significance because the model predicts that stock returns and levered investment returns should be equal at each point in time without any error term, which is not possible to achieve in the data. Nevertheless, if the model provides a reasonably description of the real world, the investment return generated by a successful candidate model should match well the behavior of the stock returns in the real data, and this matching can be assessed with standard goodness of fit measures.Specifically, we investigate how well the model is able to capture the average cross-sectional variation and the time-series variation of stock returns across portfolios. Accordingly, our analyses focuses on first stage GMM estimates. (In the online appendix we show that our conclusions are similar if we use second stage GMM.) The evaluation of the model is then based on the properties of easy-to-interpret goodness of fit measures such as the cross-sectional R2 (XS−R2) of the scatter plot of average portfolio-level stock returns against average portfolio-level estimated levered investment returns, the time-series R2 (TS−R2) of the realized stock return against the realized estimated levered investment returns of the portfolios, and the magnitude of the cross-sectional and time-series pricing errors of each portfolio.We compute the different goodness of fit measures as follows. Denote the time series average of stock return and model-implied levered investment return for each portfolio i as riS¯ and riIw¯, respectively, and define rS¯¯=1N∑i=1NriS¯. We compute the XS−R2 and the TS−R2 (with pooled data of the portfolios across time) as follows:5 (11)XS−R2=1−∑i=1NriS¯−riIw¯2∑i=1NriS¯−rS¯¯2,and (12)TS−R2=1−∑i=1N∑t=1Trit+1S−rit+1Iw2∑i=1N∑t=1Trit+1S−rS¯¯2.In addition, we compute average pricing errors, both in the cross-section and in the time-series. We define the model cross-sectional pricing error of portfolio i, which we can interpret as the standard alpha (abnormal return) in the asset pricing literature, as: (13)eiXS≡ETrit+1S−rit+1Iw,in which ET⋅ is the sample mean of the series in brackets. The mean absolute cross-sectional error across portfolios is then defined as (14)|eXS|=1N∑i=1N|eiXS|.Similarly, we compute the alpha of the spread high-minus-low portfolio (H−L, which is typically examined in the asset pricing literature) as: (15)|eH−LXS|=|eHXS−eLXS|.To obtain the pricing errors in the time-series, we first compute the time-series pricing error for each portfolio i at each point in time as: (16)eit+1TS≡rit+1S−rit+1Iw.We then define the mean absolute time-series error averaged over time and across portfolios as: (17)|eTS|=1N1T∑i=1N∑t=1T|eit+1TS|.Note that the units of all the pricing errors discussed here are in annual returns per year.In addition, if the model provides a good description of reality, the estimation residuals should be small, and largely random either across portfolios or over time, that is, they should not exhibit a systematic behavior. Hence, we also study the properties of the time-series estimation residuals (errors) implied by the estimation. In particular, we perform a principal components analysis (PCA) of the residuals of the portfolios over time, and report the fraction of the variation of the residuals that is explained by each principal component.For completeness, we also perform standard statistical tests of the model. We assume that stock and investment returns are observed with an error. The general distribution theory applies to GMM with prespecified weighting matrices (Cochrane, 2009). Let D=∂gT/∂θ. We estimate S, a consistent estimate of the variance–covariance matrix of the sample errors gT, with a standard Bartlett kernel with a lag length of two. The estimate of θ, denoted θˆ, is asymptotically normal with the following variance–covariance matrix: (18)varθˆ=1TD′WD−1D′WSWDD′WD−1.To construct standard errors for individual model errors, we use: (19)vargT=1TI−DD′WD−1D′WSI−DD′WD−1D′W′,which is the variance–covariance matrix for gT. We follow Hansen (1982) to form a χ2 test on the null hypothesis that all of the model errors are jointly zero: (20)gT′vargT+gT∼χ2#moments−#paras,in which χ2 denotes the chi-square distribution, and the superscript ＋ denotes pseudo-inversion.6
article.5. Estimation and tests results: Section 5.1 describes the data. Section 5.2 reports the estimation results of the baseline investment-based model from matching the cross-sectional moments only. Section 5.3 reports the estimation and tests results from matching the cross-sectional and the time-series moments jointly.5.1. DataTo facilitate the comparison with prior studies, our sample construction and data alignment largely follows LWZ and GXZ. Our sample consists of all common stocks traded on NYSE, Amex, and Nasdaq from 1963 to 2020. The firm-level data are from the Center for Research in Security Prices (CRSP) monthly stock files and the annual Standard and Poor’s Compustat files. We exclude firms in financial and utility sectors and firms with non positive total assets, capital, or sales. We include only firms with a fiscal year end in December to align the accounting data across firms.75.1.1. Testing portfoliosAs noted, we use ten book-to-market (BM) portfolios as the test assets. Following Fama and French (1992), at the end of June of each year t, we sort all stocks based on BM, which is defined as the book equity for the fiscal year ending in calendar year t−1 divided by the market equity (from CRSP) at the end of December of t−1, into deciles based on the NYSE breakpoints. We calculate equal-weighted annual returns from July of each year t to June of year t+1 for the portfolios, which are re-balanced at the end of each June. (In the online appendix we show that our main conclusions are similar to those reported here if we use portfolios sorted on standardized unexpected earnings, corporate investment, or asset growth as test assets, as in LWZ.)5.1.2. Variable measurement and timing alignmentCompustat records both stock and flow variables at the end of year t. In the model, however, stock variables dated t are measured at the beginning of year t, and flow variables dated t are over the course of year t. As such, for the year t=2020, for example, we take time-t stock variables from the 2019 balance sheet, and time-t flow variables from the 2020 income or cash flow statement.We measure output, Yit, as sales (item SALE). Capital stock, Kit, is net property, plant, and equipment (item PPENT). Depreciation rate, δit, is the amount of depreciation and amortization (item DP) subtracted by the amortization of intangibles (item AM, zero if missing) and then divided by the capital stock, Kit. We measure investment, Iit, directly as Kit+1−1−δitKit. Total debt, Bit+1, is long-term debt (item DLTT, zero if missing) plus short-term debt (item DLC, zero if missing). The market leverage, wit, is the ratio of total debt to the sum of total debt and market equity (from CRSP). The tax rate, τt, is the statutory corporate income tax rate from the Commerce House’s annual publications. We measure the pretax cost of debt, rit+1B, as the ratio of total interest and related expenses (item XINT) scaled by total debt, Bit+1.To mitigate the impact of outliers, we winsorize 2% of the extreme observations every year. We winsorize the unbounded variables such as investment rate, Iit/Kit, at the 1%−99% level. For variables that are bounded below at zero, such as sales-to-capital, Yit+1/Kit+1, and the depreciation rate, δit, we winsorize at the 0%−98% level. Finally, we do not winsorize the market leverage, wit, because it is bounded between zero and one by construction.To match stock returns with levered investment returns, we follow the LWZ timing convention. Portfolio stock returns are calculated from July of year t to June of year t+1. To construct the matching annual investment returns, we use capital at the end of fiscal year t−1 (Kit), the tax rate, investment, and capital at the end of year t (τt, Iit, and Kit+1), as well as other variables at the end of year t+1 (τt+1, Yit+1, Iit+1, and δit+1). Because stock variables are measured at the beginning of the year and flow variables are realized over the course of the year, the investment returns go approximately from the middle of year t to the middle of year t+1, consistent with the timing of stock returns.5.2. Matching cross-sectional moments onlyWe first estimate the investment-based model using only the cross-sectional moments given by Eq. (8), as in LWZ. article.6. Potential empirical reasons for the p: We investigate if empirical issues, in particular, portfolio-level aggregation bias and the potential misalignment between asset prices and real quantity (e.g. investment) data, might be responsible for the poor time series fit of the baseline model. As we show here through simulations, these empirical issues can generate a poor time series fit even if the baseline model is the true model. We propose solutions to address these empirical issues which we then use to re-estimate the baseline model in the real data.We only investigate empirical issues here given the focus of the paper on the estimation and testing methodology of investment-based models. In the online appendix, however, we also investigate a few potential economic reasons for the model’s poor time series fit. Specifically, we connect our findings to the well-documented failure of the q-investment regressions (e.g. Fazzari, Hubbard, and Petersen 1988) and some of its proposed explanations, in particular, the role of financial frictions. Although naturally related, we show that the poor time series fit of the model on the stock-investment return relationship that we document here goes beyond the simple failure of the q-investment regressions. When we re-estimate the model assuming in the investment return Eq. (5) that the q-investment relationship holds at each point in time, the model’s time-series fit improves, but it remains poor, with a TS−R2=20%.8 Similarly, we show that financial frictions seem to have a modest quantitative impact on the stock-investment return correlations.6.1. Aggregation biasAs noted in BGSV and GXZ, the portfolio aggregation procedure in LWZ suffers from an aggregation bias because the portfolio-level stock and investment returns, which the estimation tries to match, are not computed in a consistent manner. As a result, the parameter estimates do not have a structural interpretation and the equality between portfolio-level stock and investment returns does not have to hold. Specifically, as discussed in Section 4.1, the portfolio-level investment return in LWZ is obtained by first computing each portfolio-level characteristic separately (e.g., the portfolio-level investment rate, marginal product of capital), and then plugging each of these characteristics directly in the investment return formula to obtain the portfolio-level investment return at each point in time. In turn, the estimation matches this portfolio-level investment return to the equal-weighted average of firm-level stock returns in the portfolio. Given the non-linearity of the investment return formula, however, the LWZ aggregation procedure breaks the equality between portfolio-level stock and investment returns (unless all the firms in the portfolio have exactly the same characteristics, which is counterfactual).To properly estimate the model and recover the structural parameters, the portfolio-level stock and investment return should be computed in the same manner, as in GXZ. Specifically, given the nonlinear nature of investment returns, we first compute the investment return for each firm (indexed by the subscript j), and then compute the portfolio-level (indexed by the subscript i) investment return as the equal-weighted average of the firm-level investment returns of the firms in the portfolio as rit+1I=1Nit∑j=1Nitrijt+1I. We label this portfolio aggregation approach as proper aggregation. In turn, this should be matched with the corresponding portfolio i’s equal-weighted stock return computed as rit+1S=1Nit∑j=1Nitrijt+1S. (We focus on equal-weighted returns here to be consistent with the approach in LWZ and GXZ, but our results are similar if we use value-weighted returns.)To investigate if the aggregation bias induced by the LWZ portfolio-level aggregation can help explain the poor time-series fit of the baseline model documented in the previous section, we perform two sets of analyses. First, we use simulated data in which the model holds by construction at the firm-level, and re-estimate the model to evaluate the theoretical impact of the aggregation-bias on the model fit. Second, we do the proper aggregation in the real data, and re-estimate the model to re-evaluate its time-series fit.6.1.1. The impact of aggregation bias in simulated dataWe simulate data from a model economy in which the assumptions of the baseline investment-based model hold, and hence firm-level stock and investment returns are equal by construction. To generate the data in a simple manner, we use the real data on firm-level characteristics (such as, sales-to-capital, investment, capital stock, and depreciation rate) as inputs to construct the model-implied investment and stock returns. We set the true model parameter values for capital share as α=0.04, and the adjustment cost parameter as c=0.8, and compute the model-implied firm-level investment return using Eq. (5) and the real data on firm characteristics. According to Eq. (7), this firm-level investment return is also the firm-level stock return. As in the empirical analysis, we then create 10 BM portfolios which we use to replicate the GMM estimation of the model. The parameter values used here generate a value premium in the artificial data that is similar to the one in the real data.9To examine the theoretical impact of the aggregation bias on the evaluation of the model, we then replicate the LWZ portfolio aggregation method using the simulated data. Panel A in Table 3 confirms the aggregation bias in the parameter estimates, consistent with the results of BGSV: the estimation fails to recover the true parameter values. Another evidence of the aggregation bias is that the parameter estimates vary with the set of moments used (columns 1 to 5). The aggregation bias is also able to break the perfect correlation between stock and investment returns: according to Panel A in Table 3, the maximum TS−R2 the model can achieve is 1%. This poor fit using the simulated data gives hope that the aggregation bias alone might be an important contributor for the poor empirical time-series fit of model when estimated using the LWZ portfolio aggregation procedure.The results using the simulated data also confirm that the time-series moments have more power to detect model misspecification (here, due to the portfolio-aggregation error), than the cross-sectional moments. When the model is estimated using only the cross-sectional moments as in column (1) in Table 3, the cross sectional errors are low, the XS−R2 is high, about 51%, and we fail to reject the model by the χ2 test with a p-value on testing the joint errors of 10 cross-sectional moments of 0.52. That is, the incorrectly estimated model can easily match the cross-sectional moments. In contrast, when the model is estimated using the time-series moments as in column (5) in Table 3, both the cross-sectional fit and time-series fit of the model are poor, and the p-value of the χ2 test on the joint errors of 10 time-series moments is only 0.17, indicating a poor model fit stemming from, in this case, the portfolio-level aggregation bias.Table 3. Misalignment between asset prices and quantity dataThe poor time series fit of the baseline investment-based model might also arise because of the potential misalignment between asset prices and quantity (e.g. investment) data. For example, stock prices (and hence stock returns) might respond instantaneously to aggregate shocks, whereas firm investment might take more time to adjust, in which case investment returns lag stock returns (see, for example, Lamont 2000, for a more formal analysis of this issue). But if the misalignment in the data is relatively short lived, the misalignment should be less relevant for longer-horizon returns (i.e., for time-smoothed data).6.2.1. The impact of data misalignment in simulated dataWe use simulated data to test the conjecture that compounded multi-year (here, using a five-year horizon) stock and investment returns should be less affected by data misalignment issues than one year horizon returns.As in the previous sub-section, we first generate simulated data from a specification of the model in which the equality between stock and investment returns holds (using the same parameter values as in the previous analysis). We then introduce misalignment in the simulated data as follows. If investment lags stock returns in the real data, we can capture this feature in the simulated data in a simple manner by matching stock returns at t+1 (rit+1S) with lagged investment returns at t (ritIw), and use this timing to construct the cross-sectional and the time-series moments in the GMM estimation of the model. To isolate the impact of data misalignment on the time series fit, we estimate the model using the proper portfolio-level aggregation method.Panel B in Table 3 shows that this data misalignment alone breaks significantly the perfect time-series fit of the model. The mean absolute time series error becomes sizable (all above 13% across columns 6 to 10), and the TS−R2 is very low, 10.9% (in column 10, even when the model is estimated on time-series moments only), which is more consistent with the data. Thus, theoretically, this data misalignment is another possible explanation for the poor empirical time-series fit of the model using annual return data.We then test the conjecture that using (annualized) compounded long-horizon returns in the estimation mitigates the impact of short-term data misalignment issues, and hence improves the time-series fit of the model. To verify this conjecture, we perform the estimation of the model using (annualized) 5-year compounded stock and investment returns to compute the cross-sectional and time-series moments. In particular, (annualized) 5-year compounded stock and investment returns are computed from the misaligned annual stock and investment return data as rit+1Srit+2S...rit+5S1/5 and ritIwrit+1Iw...rit+4Iw1/5, respectively. Panel C in Table 3 reports the estimation results. The TS−R2 increases significantly to around 70.6% (in column 15), and the mean absolute time series error becomes smaller, consistent with our conjecture.6.2.2. The impact of data misalignment in real dataWe re-estimate the baseline investment-based model in the real data using (annualized) 5-year compounded stock and investment returns to compute the cross-sectional and time-series moments. Panel B in Table 4 reports the results. When we use long horizon compounded returns in the estimation, the time-series fit of the model remains poor, with a TS−R2=−10.5% (see column 10, even when only the time-series moments are used in the estimation).Taken together, the results in this section show that portfolio-level aggregation-bias and the potential misalignment between asset prices and quantity data (at least the type of misalignment specification examined here) do not appear to be the main causes for the inability of the baseline investment-based model to capture the time-series behavior of stock returns in the real data.
article.7. Testing a broader class of models: The analysis so far has been restricted to the specification of the investment-based model in which the Hayashi (1982) conditions (operating profit and adjustment cost functions with constant returns to scale) holds. In this case, stock returns and (levered) investment returns should be equal at each point in time, and we use this result to estimate the model.10 Although these Hayashi conditions should be interpreted as approximations, they are nevertheless strong assumptions that are not satisfied by a large class of investment-based models. For example, the Hayashi conditions, and hence the stock-investment return equality, might not hold in models with decreasing returns to scale, fixed adjustment and operating costs, and time-to-build, which might limit the scope of our approach. It is also natural to question the validity of the constant returns to scale technology assumption given the poor time-series fit of the baseline investment-based model in the data.7.1. A flexible evaluation approachHere, we propose a more general approach to evaluate the performance of investment-based asset pricing modelsthat can be used in practice even when the potentially restrictive Hayashi conditions, and hence the stock-investment return equality, do not hold. We label this more general method as flexible evaluation approach because it does not rely so heavily on functional form restrictions. As a result, this approach is useful to test the asset pricing implications of a broader set of specifications of the investment-based model.In any investment-based model, it is natural to investigate the link between a firm’s equilibrium stock return and its characteristics (analogous to the stock-investment return equality in the baseline model). We can determine the strength of these links in the model and in the real data using simple linear regressions of the form: (21)ritS=βXit,in which Xi is a vector of firm (or portfolio-level) characteristics.11 This stock return-firm characteristics regression can be estimated both in the time-series (using panel data), and in the cross-section (using the time series average of stock returns and firm characteristics). In the baseline neoclassical model with one (physical) capital input and in which the Hayashi conditions hold, the key firm characteristics Xit in the investment return formula in Eq. (5) include current profitability (sales-to-capital ratio), and current and lagged physical capital investment rate. In other investment-based models, the key relevant firm characteristics might be different, but as long as the firm characteristics can be measured both in the data and in the model, we can evaluate if a given model generates a relationship between realized stock returns and firm characteristics that is consistent with the data (in terms of goodness of fit and slope coefficients).The stock return-firm characteristics regression can be used to evaluate investment-based models in at least two ways. First, it can be used as out-of-sample moments to evaluate the cross-sectional and time-series implications for stock returns of a given model’s calibration. We follow this approach here. More generally, the stock return-firm characteristics regressions can be used to simultaneously estimate the structural parameters and test a given model. The estimation can be done using alternative estimation methods such as the Simulated Method of Moments (SMM), adding the stock return-firm characteristics regression results estimated in the real data as an additional set of target moments for model estimation.Similar approaches have been proposed in the q-investment literature. Eberly et al. (2008) show that when the Hayashi conditions do not hold, Tobin’s q is not a sufficient statistic for investment, but the optimal investment from a model featuring decreasing returns to scale and a fixed cost can still be well approximated by a log-linear function of q. Gala et al. (2020) show that even under very general assumptions about the nature of markets, production and investment technologies, optimal investments are functions of and well captured by the relevant state variables such as firm size and productivity.7.2. The flexible evaluation approach in practiceTo show how the flexible evaluation approach can be used in practice, we estimate the time-series and cross-sectional relationships between stock returns and firm characteristics defined in Eq. (21) both in the real data, and also in simulated data generated from the following three different specifications of investment-based models with one (physical) capital input. (In the online appendix, we provide a detailed description of the setup and calibration of these models).Baseline investment-based model. The first model is the baseline investment-based model in which the Hayashi conditions, and hence the stock-investment return equality, hold. We generate data from this model in the same way as in the previous Section 6. This specification of the model is useful here as a benchmark because we already know that in this model stock returns and firm characteristics are closely linked by Eq. (5). We can then investigate if this nonlinear relationship is preserved in the linear approximation of this relationship captured by the stock return-firm characteristics regressions.LZ model. The second model is an off-the-shelf investment-based model from Lin and Zhang (2013) with decreasing returns to scale, non convex and asymmetric adjustment costs, and operating fixed costs, in which the Hayashi conditions, and hence the stock-investment return equality, do not hold. We consider this model given its good fit on matching key moments of real quantities and asset prices in simulated data, and the more general specification of the firm’s technology relative to the baseline model.KP model. The third model is the non-adjustment cost model of Kogan and Papanikolaou (2013) with investment-specific shocks, in which the stock-investment return equality also does not hold. We consider this model given its ability to explain a large number of patterns in the cross section of average stock returns, and the significantly different model setup and assumptions relative to the baseline neoclassical model.12To estimate Eq. (21), we consider the following firm-characteristics: profitability (YKit≡YitKit), investment rate (IKit≡IitKit) and its squared term, lagged investment rate (IKit−1≡Iit−1Kit−1), and size (Kit−1≡logKit−1) at each point in time, and also in the cross-section, in which case we average all variables (stock returns and characteristics) over time for each portfolio. Most of these variables are motivated by the key firm-characteristics in the investment-return formula of the baseline one (physical) capital input model in Eq. (5), where we add firm-size to capture the possible effect of decreasing returns to scale. Consistent with the previous analyses, we run the regressions at the portfolio-level (using 10 book-to-market portfolios) to reduce noise in the firm-level data, and in which each portfolio-level characteristic is computed as the equal-weighted average of the characteristic across firms in the portfolio. Since the LZ and KP models do not feature leverage, we use unlevered stock returns in the real data and in the baseline model in all the analyses here to allow for a meaningful comparison. We unlever portfolio stock returns in the data by multiplying levered returns with one minus portfolio-level average leverage. We normalize both the dependent and the independent variables using pooled data to facilitate the comparisons.Table 5 reports the stock return-firm characteristics regression results in the three models (columns 1 to 6) using simulated data, and in the real data (columns 7 and 8). In the baseline investment-based model, columns (1) and (2) show that the tight (non-linear) relationship between stock returns and firm characteristics predicted by the model (the stock-investment return equality) is preserved in the linear approximation, given the high XS−R2 of 91%, and the high TS−R2 of 90%.In the LZ and KP models, columns (3) to (6) show that, even though these models do not predict the stock-investment return equality, these models also imply that stock returns and firm-characteristics are highly correlated, both in the cross-section (XS−R2 above 96% in both models), and in the time-series (TS−R2 above 84% in both models). These results show that the strong link between stock returns and firm characteristics (at least using data aggregated at the portfolio-level) is not restricted to specifications of investment-based models in which the Hayashi conditions hold. In addition, the very high cross-sectional and time-series R2 in these regressions mean that these models predict that the firm-characteristics that we consider here are key drivers of stock returns.To evaluate these models we compare the stock return-firm characteristic regression results in each model, with those in the real data. Columns (7) and (8) show that, consistent with the cross-sectional regressions in the three models, the cross-sectional fit in the real data is quite high, about 76%. Inconsistent with these model’s predictions, however, the time-series fit of the stock return-firm characteristics regression in the real data is very poor, with a TS−R2 of about 5%, and the coefficients on the firm-characteristics that we investigate here are mostly insignificant. The strong time series fit of the stock return-firm characteristics regressions in these models, but poor fit in the real data, suggests that the set of firm characteristics driving stock returns in one (physical) capital investment-based models differs from those in the real data. This result suggests that important firm-characteristics might be missing in these models.Table 5. article.8. Conclusion and implications for futur: Investment-based asset pricing models typically predict a close link between a firm’s stock return and its characteristics at any point in time, not just on average, as examined in previous studies. We show how to incorporate the time-series predictions in the estimation and testing of investment-based models using the GMM. In contrast with previous studies that find that standard specifications of investment-based asset pricing models with one (physical) capital input can match the cross section of stock returns quite well, we find that these specifications struggle to match the time series of stock returns observed in the data.Our findings highlight the importance of examining the time-series implications of investment-based models for proper model evaluation. Because we only test a limited set of specifications of investment-based models with one (physical) capital input, our findings do not imply that the investment-based paradigm cannot match the time-series of stock return data well. Our findings suggest, however, that a different specification of the model, and/or a change in the econometric procedures, is needed to capture the time-series dimension of stock returns in the real data, which we argue is an important dimension to match. Going forward, additional capital inputs such as intangible capital as in Peters and Taylor (2017) and BGSV, quasi-fixed labor inputs as in BGSV, or short-term and long-term assets as in GXZ, might be added to the model. In addition, accounting for firm- or industry-level heterogeneity in the technologies, which are assumed to be similar across firms in the baseline analyses, as well as more general functional forms, should be investigated. It could also be useful to examine further the impact of financial frictions as in Gomes et al. (2006), or time-to-build features as in Kuehn (2009), on the correlation between realized stock returns and firm characteristics at both short- and long-horizons. Finally, a broad assessment of the impact of measurement error as in Erickson and Whited (2000) on the time-series moments should be investigated.
